text: Welcome. This demonstrating video is about the five standardized steps for robotic left upper lobectomy with robotic stage.

The surgery was performed for a sixty-three-year-old man with smoking history, emphysema and CEOS in whom we discovered a central over nodule in the left upper lobe which fixes on TEP scanner. We used a surgical approach using four robotic arms, introduced through four trocars of eight millimeters each and one utility incision of twelve millimeters.
The dissection was according to anticlock dissection. Every step is summarized with the main surgical time and operating mean duration.

The surgeon begins step one of the robotic left upper lobectomy, performing dissection in an anticlockwise direction starting from the pulmonary ligament zone. The pulmonary ligament is exposed and sectioned, followed by lymphadenectomy of lymph nodes at station nine.

The surgeon approaches zone II, exposing the posterior hilum to access the posterior side of the left pulmonary vein and mediastinal artery. The same exposure is used to perform lymph node dissection at stations VII and X.

The surgeon accesses the upper hilum side, exposing the superior side of the mediastinal artery. Lymphadenectomy is performed at stations ten, five, and six, with visible dissection of lymph nodes in these areas.
The surgeon retracts the lung towards the posterior pleural side to access the anterior hilum, facilitating dissection of the mediastinal artery and lymph nodes in stations ten and eleven.
The surgeon performs a complete lymph node dissection at stations eleven and twelve, controlling the artery, vein, and bronchus using robotic instruments before sectioning the structures.
Following the bronchus transection, the surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy to gain access to the pulmonary artery, carefully dissecting surrounding lymph nodes.

The surgeon applies a nail veil sheet to the sutured lung zone to ensure aerostasis. After the complete resection, the lung specimen is placed into an angiobag for extraction. Finally, a pleural chest tube is inserted, marking the conclusion of the procedure.

Post-operative course was unfeintful and the patient was discharged at day three after surgery with normal chest radiography. Pathology concluded to a one point two centimeter adenocarcinoma with no gene mutations and no lymph node metastasis.
